com.mentor.chs.plugin.action

Interface IXAction

  • All Superinterfaces:
    IXAvailability, IXBaseAction, IXPlugin
    All Known Subinterfaces:
    IXApplicationAction, IXFunctionDesignAction, IXHarnessAction, IXIntegratorAction, IXLogicAction, IXPlatformArchitectureDesignAction, IXSBOMAction, IXTopologyAction

    @NotToBeImplementedDirectly
public interface IXAction
extends IXBaseAction, IXAvailability
    Parent class for all Custom Actions in Design Tools. You should not implement this class directly. You should implement one of its children, for example, IXLogicAction, IXIntegratorAction or IXHarnessAction. This will allow Capital to associate your plug-in with the correct design type. If you have developed a Custom Action that can be used with more than one design type, all you need to do is implement all the interfaces concerned.

    The name used on the menu entry will be the string returned by the IXPlugin.getName() method.

    NOT TO BE USED DIRECTLY.

    This interface extends the IXAvailability interface. If your plugins should only be available when a single object is selected: 
               public boolean isAvailable(IXApplicationContext context)
           {
                   return context.getSelectedObjects().size() == 1;
           }
    For Harness designs, when you select a single object on the design, it is possible that two objects are actually selected. If you select a clip, for example, the clip object will be selected and the node object the clip is associated with.
    See Also:
    IXLogicAction, IXIntegratorAction, IXHarnessAction, IXApplicationContext

      static class  IXAction.Trigger
      Used to indicate where the Custom Action can be triggered from.

      boolean execute(IXApplicationContext applicationContext)

    • Method Detail

      • execute

        boolean execute(IXApplicationContext applicationContext)
        This is the method that actually does the work for your action.

        This is the method that will be called when the custom action's menu entry is clicked. This method must return a Boolean that indicates the success or failure of the custom action. This method need not open a dialog. However, if a dialog is opened, it should be opened modal, for example: 

            boolean execute(IXApplicationContext applicationContext) {
                JDialog dialog = new JDialog(applicationContext.getParentFrame(), true);
      dialog.setTitle(getName());
      //...
      // create your dialog contents here...
      //...
      dialog.setVisible(true);
      return true;
    }

        For compatibility with future releases of Capital, please try to return true or false if your dialog has been OKed or CANCELed, or return false if the operation failed, true if the operation succeeded. If you have made changes to properties/attributes, those changes will NOT be applied unless you return 'true'.

        WARNING: If you have to use frames, NEVER NEVER EVER set the default close option to JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE: 

            JFrame frame = ...
    frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // This is BAD
        This will cause the application to exit when you close the frame. This exit will cause the loss of any unsaved data. DO NOT DO THIS. You should use a JDialog or do the following explictly: 
            JFrame frame = ...
    frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE); // This is BETTER
        Parameters:
        applicationContext - - the application context. From this object, the currently selected objects can be obtained. Also the current design and/or project.
        Returns:
        should return true, if the action was completed successfully , otherwise false.
